title: UConn vs. Illinois box score: Full stats from 2026 Final Four basketball game
description: Here's a look at the complete box scores for both UConn and Illinois from Saturday's Final Four matchup.
fullText:
UConn vs. Illinois box score: Full stats from 2026 Final Four basketball game originally appeared on The Sporting News. Add The Sporting News as a Preferred Source by clicking here.

After 40 minutes, the No. 2 UConn Huskies are heading back to their third title game in four seasons.

The Huskies took care of the No. 3 Fighting Illini 71-62 in a game that showed shooting troubles for both teams. The Huskies shot only 35% from the field and 36% from beyond the arc. Illinois couldn't meet either of these marks, shooting 34% from the field along with a 23% mark from three.

Fighting Illini star freshman Keaton Wagler led all scorers with 20 points (7/16 FG, 2/10 3P), along with pitching in eight rebounds and two assists. Despite not scoring much in the second half, Tarris Reed Jr. and Braylon Mullins led the scoring for the Huskies. Reed earned his third double-double of this year's tournament with 17 points (6/12 FG) and 11 boards, while Mullins shot 5/14 (4/7 3P) for 15 points, along with three assists and two steals. Despite scoring in single digits, junior guard Silas Demary Jr. filled the score sheet. He finished with seven points (2/8 FG, 1/6 3P), nine rebounds, seven assists, and two steals en route to victory.

UConn will take on the victor of No. 1 Arizona and No. 1 Michigan on Monday at the home field of the Indianapolis Colts.

Here's a look at the complete box scores for both UConn and Illinois from Saturday's Final Four matchup.

title: Brayden Burries’s Parents Are Both Former College Athletes
description: Here's what to know about basketball star Brayden Burries and his parents, Hannah and Bobby, who both played college sports.
fullText:
"Hearst Magazines and Yahoo may earn commission or revenue on some items through these links."

Brayden Burries has only been playing college basketball for a year and he’s already one of the biggest stars in the NCAA Men’s Final Four this year. Brayden grew up in San Bernardino, California, attending Riverside Polytechnic High School before transferring to Eleanor Roosevelt High School after his freshman year. As a senior, he won the California CIF State Basketball Championship with Roosevelt and was selected to play in the 2025 McDonald's All-American Boys Game. After some wooing from multiple college programs, he now plays for the Arizona Wildcats of the Big 12 Conference.

The 2026 NBA draft is expected to take place in late June. In a recent mock draft from USA Today, Brayden is already considered so talented he could go in the first round. (That doesn’t mean he has confirmed or announced anything yet.) “Burries has also looked like a lottery talent during March Madness, earning a spot in the Sweet 16,” wrote Bryan Kalbrosky, USA Today’s senior NBA staff writer. “He has proven productivity and he is able to defend, relocate, move the ball and make 3-pointers off the dribble.”

Brayden comes from a sports household, and both of his parents are often seen at his Arizona games. Below, get to know Brayden’s parents, Hannah and Bobby Burries.

Brayden’s mother, Hannah, played two seasons of collegiate softball at Tennessee and one season of basketball at Cal State San Bernardino as a graduate transfer, earning All-Conference honors and a degree in business administration. It was at Cal State San Bernardino that she met her husband, Bobby, who played on the men’s basketball team. They have been together 21 years and have five children, all with “B” names: BJ, Brianna, Brayden, Brealani, and Brielle.

After college, Hannah was a teacher for six years before joining United Airlines, where she has worked for nearly two decades. She was awarded the United 100 award in 2017, given annually to only 100 employees companywide for exceptional performance, and received the honor again in 2023.

Bobby was also a star athlete in school. He started out playing both football and basketball in high school before focusing on college basketball at San Bernardino Valley College and Cal State San Bernardino, where he is now a member of the latter’s Hall of Fame. He went on to play professionally overseas. Brayden has credited his father with instilling a winning mentality in him from an early age. “That’s helped me get to the point where I’m at today,” Brayden said in 2025.

title: March Madness: Illinois falls to UConn in Final Four, ending Illini team's longest run in 21 years
description: Illinois was knocked out of the NCAA men's basketball March Madness tournament Saturday, ending the Fighting Illini team's longest run in 21 years. Fans had showed up big in Indianapolis and Champaign.
fullText:
The University of Illinois was knocked out of the NCAA men's basketball March Madness tournament Saturday in Indianapolis, ending the Fighting Illini team's longest run in 21 years.

UConn got another critical 3-pointer from Braylon Mullins and coach Dan Hurley's Huskies are heading back to the national title game, beating Illinois 71-62 in the Final Four on Saturday night as they seek their third championship in four seasons.

Tarris Reed Jr. had 17 points and 11 rebounds, while the fabulous freshman Mullins scored 15 for the Huskies (34-5), who rode strong inside play and tough defense to their 19th straight victory in the Sweet 16 or later rounds of the NCAA Tournament.

UConn will face Arizona or Michigan on Monday night as it seeks its seventh title, all since 1999, and third under Hurley, who would become the only active coach with more than two.

Mullins, whose buzzer-beating 3-pointer sent the Huskies past Duke and into the Final Four, hit a 3 with 52 seconds left that gave UConn a 66-59 lead.

Freshman Keaton Wagler had 20 points and eight rebounds to lead the Fighting Illini (28-9), who reached their first Final Four since losing the championship game to North Carolina in 2005.

Despite the heartbreaking loss for Illinois, Illini fans were making the most of watch parties earlier in the day Saturday.

The party started early for fans in Indianapolis. Meanwhile, former players, fans and students on campus in Champaign, Illinois said the anticipation was high.

With the Illini band and cheerleaders leading the way, the Fighting Illini nation at Lucas Oil Stadium was pumped up. While there might have been four schools there Saturday, Illinois was taking it over before the Final Four games began.

"It's like a home game. Everybody's wearing orange, and Indianapolis feels like Champaign to me," said Brad Lane with the Chicago Illini Club.

University of Illinois fans flooded Indianapolis for their Final Four March Madness game against UConn.

There seemed to be an endless line of orange-clad Illinois fans walking into pre-parties before the main event at Lucas Oil Stadium.

"We have by far the most amount of fans here from any team," said Matty Kramer with The Boardroom Podcast.

Tracy Abrams played and graduated in 2017.

"We never got to do this," Abrams said. "Just to be here supporting these guys, I'm with it. I'm all in!"

Marcus Griffin played and graduated in 2001.

"Just for all the players that came before," Griffin said. "This is all we ever wanted to see, wanted to see coach and the players get to a national championship."

Final Four March Madness watch parties were sold on the University of Illinois campus in Champaign.

Excitement was also building on the campus of the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign prior to the game.

An Illini Union watch party got underway Saturday afternoon, where eager fans had hoped for a win.

"It's history in the making, since 2005," U of I alum Dwayne Banks, Jr. said. "It's crazy, and it's crazy to see."

Students and alumni gathered in the Illini Union to witness the Fighting Illini basketball team's historic winning streak before it came to an end in the Final Four.

"Didn't really see this coming at the start of the tournament, but I mean, we've just been rolling," U of I sophomore student Vivin Anand said.

"It's just great to have an ongoing legacy of Illini just bleeding orange and blue with everybody," U of I class of 1989 alum Lori Nixon said.

"We're old enough that we've seen the Flying Illini in action. We had season tickets back in the day, and this is a big moment," U of I class of 1989 alum Bruce Nixon said.

For most students, they weren't even born the last time the team made it to the Final Four in 2005.

"It's something we've never seen before," U of I sophomore student Ayan Bhakta said. "We've been here for two years. There hasn't been a win, like the Elite Eight. And, we're just going to keep going."

The hype behind the team was palpable beyond campus, too. ABC7 saw lines stretching out of nearby bars, like Legends, where watch parties were sold out. At some places, it was $100 or more a ticket. And even then, a ticket did not guarantee a spot inside.

"We're trying to get in. So, we're at the end of the line, like you said, so hopefully we make it in there," U of I alum Giovanni Guzman said. "It's time to celebrate and make sure that we can, at least, enjoy a great game and make some history, too. Let's go! U of I, let's go!"

RELATED | March Madness: Illinois team, fans make final preparations for Final Four game in Indianapolis

The Associated Press contribute to this report.

title: "We've Never Wavered:" Oilers Defend Belief In Their Group
description: We should've believed them.
fullText: Every time an Edmonton Oiler stepped in front of a mic this season and said they had belief in their room, that they knew what they were capable of, we nodded and moved on. Connor McDavid said it, Leon Draisaitl said it, Ryan Nugent-Hopkins, Zach Hyman, take your pick. The message never changed. Now here's Darnell Nurse saying it again. Nagging Injury Forces Oilers To Pull Zach Hyman Heading Into Playoffs The Edmonton Oilers are prioritizing playoff health, resting star scorer Zach Hyman to ensure he's as close to 100 percent for the postseason. "We've never wavered in what we believe that we're capable of in here, and that's the special part of this group," said Nurse. Hard to argue. The Oilers are tied for first in the Pacific, with a shot at home ice. That's not something anyone was saying with confidence a few weeks ago, including Kris Knoblauch. "We're in danger of not making the playoffs," the head coach said. Out loud. To the media. Did The Oilers Bail On A 24-Goal Bruins Forward Too Soon? Viktor Arvidsson is thriving in Boston, while the Oilers watch a 24-goal scorer's resurgence. Did they let him go too soon? If the guy behind the bench is willing to say it, you'd think the rest of us were allowed to have some doubts, too. And they were warranted. Edmonton spent big chunks of this season looking like a team allergic to consistency. There were some good games, but it was mostly stretches of nothing, a style of hockey that made it hard to know what to expect night to night. This five-game win streak they're riding right now? Longest one they've put together all season. Not exactly a confidence-inspiring stat. But they're finding ways to win games. Low-event, low-scoring, tight defensive hockey. Not always pretty. Probably not always deserved. But the results are there, and that's what counts this late in the season. Oilers Vs Golden Knights: Pre-game Stats 39-28-9 | 87 PTS "We're playing good hockey recently. You're not going to have your A game every night, we probably won with our B game the other night, and that's a good sign," added Nurse. "But for us to be a team (and to) play as long as we want to play, there's going to be nights when you're not feeling it, there nights when you are feeling it, you gotta get the results either." That's a guy who gets what April hockey actually demands. You don't need to be dominant every night. You need points and Edmonton is getting them. The division race isn't over. Anaheim has been a surprise this season, but the Oilers are in the driver's seat, and with this group, that's not a position they're unfamiliar with. Same core that went to the Stanley Cup Final twice. Same guys who spent all season telling us not to worry. They've been in pressure situations before and figured it out. Matt Savoie Is Making The Oilers Look Good The Edmonton Oilers beat the Chicago Blackhawks 3-1 last night. Savoie scored the winner on the power play, put five shots on net, and walked away with the first star. Not a bad night. Turns out they were right. Again.

title: Michigan's Yaxel Lendeborg Credits His Mother With Turning His Life Around
description: Here's what to know about the Michigan Wolverines forward and the parents who shaped his path to Ann Arbor.
fullText:
"Hearst Magazines and Yahoo may earn commission or revenue on some items through these links."

Yaxel Lendeborg had a decision to make in the spring of 2025: enter the NBA draft as a projected first-round pick or return to college for one more season. He chose Michigan, which reportedly offered $3 million in NIL compensation. At the same time, he reportedly turned down a much higher offer from Kentucky, reportedly in the range of $7 million to $9 million. The gamble on one more college season proved well-founded as Michigan made it to the 2026 NCAA Men’s Final Four, and he went on to become a consensus All-American and the Big Ten Player of the Year with the Wolverines.

Born September 30, 2002, in Puerto Rico, Yaxel moved to Ohio with his family at age two before settling in New Jersey at age eight. He began his college career at Arizona Western before transferring to University of Alabama at Birmingham (UAB) in 2023. In April 2025, he transferred again to Michigan. He entered the 2025 NBA draft process shortly after, was projected as a late first-round or early second-round pick—a range that carried a potential four-year rookie contract worth around $14 million—before withdrawing in May.

In a recent essay for The Players’ Tribune, titled “How My Mom Saved My Life,” Yaxel wrote about how his mother’s efforts especially guided him on the long and hard but ultimately successful path to Ann Arbor, Michigan.

“Because there were literally hundreds of times over the years when my mom could’ve given up on me. Times when, honestly, she would’ve had every right to give up on me,” Yaxel wrote. “But she never did. And now here I am. Here we are. And now that we’re here??? Man…I just want to be the kind of person who was worth not giving up on. I just want to be the player she always believed in me to become.”

Below, get to know Yaxel’s parents, Yissel Raposo and Okary Lendeborg.

Yaxel's father, Pedro, played basketball for the Dominican Republic national team. A job opportunity eventually brought the family from Puerto Rico to Pennsauken Township, New Jersey, just across the river from Philadelphia. But Okary has said in interviews that Yaxel and his siblings spent much of their time with their mother while he was working. “I would work and she would be stuck with him and every day they were together,” Okary told USA Today through a translator about Yaxel and his mother. “That just became his role model.”

Yissel Raposo, Yaxel’s mother, played volleyball for both the Dominican Republic national team and the American University of Puerto Rico. She is also the person that Yaxel credits as most responsible for turning his life around.

Growing up, Yaxel preferred baseball and video games to schoolwork. After the family lost his grandfather not long after moving to New Jersey, he retreated further, spending long days playing video games at a friend’s house. By his senior year of high school, Yissel staged an intervention and formed a plan developed with the help of his high school guidance counselors. He would enroll at Camden Community College, get his grades up, and try out for the basketball team. There would be no friends, no distractions.

“You will have to sacrifice, but I will, too,” she told him, per CNN, describing the morning routine she committed to getting him to school: waking early to get her three daughters out the door before driving Yaxel to Camden and heading to work. He followed her instructions, rallied academically, and played basketball during his senior year. Yissel then successfully lobbied to land him a spot at Arizona Western.

However, just before this last season began, Yissel was diagnosed with appendix cancer, limiting the games she has been able to attend. On the days when chemotherapy leaves her exhausted, she has found her own way to stay close to the court. “I watch Yaxel play, and that makes me so happy,” she told CNN. “I get nervous before the game, but when he plays, I'm so excited. It's a feeling of everything in my body. Just everything.”

Despite the nerves, and her health, she has said she still intends to travel to Indianapolis for the Final Four. Yaxel has tried to convince her to rest. “She’s a little disobedient,” he told CNN. “I tell her to stay away, to rest, but she doesn’t listen.”

Yaxel has repeatedly said his mother's belief in him never wavered, even when his own did. “Yaxel never believed in himself growing up,” Yissel told the Associated Press during the 2026 NCAA Men’s Tournament. “I always talked to him in a positive way, telling him he was talented and he could make it in basketball. I feel so happy and proud that now he knows he is good, too.”

title: GIRLS BB: Winamac's Kroft headlines All-Loganland team
description: Kandace Kroft broke multiple school records for the Winamac girls basketball team this season. She broke program records in career assists (363), career 3-pointers (222), single-season 3-pointers (97) and single-game 3-pointers (eight). The 5-foot-7 senior guard will graduate as Winamac’s fourth all-time leading scorer in program history with 1,214 points, joining a 1,000-point club that ...
fullText:
Kandace Kroft broke multiple school records for the Winamac girls basketball team this season.

She broke program records in career assists (363), career 3-pointers (222), single-season 3-pointers (97) and single-game 3-pointers (eight).

The 5-foot-7 senior guard will graduate as Winamac’s fourth all-time leading scorer in program history with 1,214 points, joining a 1,000-point club that includes Tiffany Crist (1993 grad, 1,374 points), Kara Crawford (2007, 1,245), Erika Redweik (2009, 1,226), Kroft (2026, 1,214), Stephanie Shorter (2014, 1,146), Shallon Bowden (1999, 1,112) and Jordan Brandt (2010, 1,108).

Kroft averaged 18.8 ppg, 6.5 rpg, 5.1 apg and 3.2 spg for a Winamac team that went 19-7. She is the Pharos-Tribune’s Loganland Player of the Year.

“I thought we had a really good season,” Kroft said. “We had a tough ending, but I was glad that we made it as far as we did because I think we struggled a little towards the end of the season. So making it to the sectional championship, it was a huge accomplishment. I’m just excited to continue to see the team improve. It was just the first four years under Coach Stesiak, so I know they’re gonna do great things in the future.”

Winamac coach Tony Stesiak took the Winamac job four years ago as Kroft was entering high school. The Warriors went 4-20 the first year, 14-10 in each of the next two years and 19-7 this year.

“She has had a tremendous career, a baptism by fire from her freshman year being the starting point guard from Day One,” Stesiak said. “We all had some growing pains that first year. And just to see her evolution, maturity and development through four years has been special.”

Stesiak previously coached the Rochester girls for 17 seasons and won a Class 2A state title in 2004 and a Class 3A regional title in 2015.

He said Kroft is up there among the best players he’s coached.

“She’s one of the best players I’ve ever coached and one of the toughest competitors, high basketball IQ, is all about winning,” he said. “That’s the thing that stuck out. Everything that she does is about trying to be the best she can be and the best team that we could be. She’s not necessarily a rah-rah type leader, but if you paid attention, as I always would tell especially this year’s freshmen, I hope you’re paying attention to how she does things because this is how you get to the as good as she is.”

Kroft will continue her career at Franklin College. The Grizzlies went 21-7 and finished runner-up in the HCAC this season.

“I’m really excited. I had a visit in early October and I’ve talked to the coaches and realized this is probably the place for me,” Kroft said. “They offer so many opportunities without basketball and obviously I’m not going to probably play basketball after college, so it was good to see the opportunities they would offer me after college. But I’ve also met some of the girls on the team and I’m just excited to jump into that chapter.”

Kroft also excels in the classroom. She has a weighted GPA of 4.048 and is ranked seventh in her senior class. She plans on studying business and marketing at Franklin.

Kroft is not the only great shooter in her family. The Winamac 3-point records she broke this year were originally set by her older sister, Kingsley, who she just missed playing alongside as Kingsley graduated four years ago as Kandace was about to enter high school.

“I think being able to watch her growing up made me want to get the records even more,” Kandace said. “But she taught me so much and seeing how good of a 3-point shooter that she was, I think it just motivated me to be better as it does sometimes in the family. But she was very supportive through everything, so I was just glad to have her there cheering for me.”

Their father, Kole Kroft, was the Winamac girls coach for five seasons following Jim Swaney and Jeff Wagner’s run as coach. Kole Kroft took a new day job four years ago and stepped aside, and Stesiak was hired.

Kandace Kroft broke Winamac school records and helped keep the program going strong over the last four years.

“She certainly left her mark on the Winamac Lady Warrior basketball program, which is tough to do considering all the great players that have come through Winamac,” Stesiak said. “I’ve coached against most of them, she’s the first one I’ve coached. She’s left her mark in the record books, but to see her development over four years and what she’s meant to our team and our program has truly been special. And like I told her, the best compliment I can give her is it’s gonna be hard for me to give No. 3 to anybody as long as I’m here just because of all the things that she’s meant to the program. I don’t know if retire the number is the right way to say, but it’s been an honor to be her coach. She’s just truly a special player and special person and I’m just honored to be able to say I was her coach at one point.”

The following is the rest of the 2026 All-Loganland girls basketball team:

The 5-foot-6 junior guard was a stat-sheet stuffer all season for the Comets. She averaged 16.5 ppg, 8.1 rpg, 3.8 apg and 3.7 spg and has already eclipsed the 1,000-point milestone with one full season left to play.

“I thought she showed tremendous growth this year as a player on the court,” Caston coach Josh Douglass said. “She upped her scoring average, upped her rebound average, upped to her assist average, upped her steals average. She took it to another level this year for herself on the court, showed great leadership on the court and did a lot for us. But what really impressed me was I think she had 27 assists last year and she ended the year with 90 this year. So finding teammates and teammates knocking down open shots which helped us become a better overall team this last year.”

The Comets went 11-13 and are not very far away from being much better. They’ll graduate just 0.5 ppg.

“We lost six games by five points or less,” coach Douglass said. “I mean, that’s almost a 17-win season.”

Cass County’s Lilly Scholarship winner is also gifted and talented on the basketball court. The 5-7 senior guard averaged 12.8 ppg, 7.1 rpg and 4.0 apg for the Panthers.

The Panthers went 10-16 and finished runner-up in their sectional with a 33-32 loss to West Central in the championship game.

“She just did everything we needed her to do,” Pioneer coach David McWherter said. “Relentless defender, improved on rebounding and that’s even with Liz (Kennell) on the court who averaged about 10 rebounds a game. Mia just did a great job for us all the way around. Great leadership — her leadership, her value to our team this year was just immeasurable. She put up 30 points against Winamac when they face guarded her most of the game. She went 8 for 14 from 3 that game, just an unbelievable game for her. And I think she really probably played her best game of the year might’ve been the Lewis Cass game that we lost. She had a double-double of 16 points and 15 rebounds. All season long she just carried us.”

The Kings’ track and cross country star also shined on the basketball court, averaging 11.2 ppg and 2.4 spg.

She’ll graduate as Cass’ sixth all-time leading scorer. The list includes Betsy Bowser (2003, 1,438), Angie Cree (1991, 1,434), Sharon Schreyer (1977, 1,163), Kendal Johnson (2022, 1,143), Savanna Thomson (2017, 1,099) and Griffin (2026, 1,062).

“She shot the ball extremely well. She had 45 3s on the season,” Cass coach Kyle Amor said. “She was four 3s away from the school record for 3s in a season, so just an incredible year. And she didn’t play in a game and a half because of her hand. At the holiday tournament she broke her hand — at least I think it was broke — and she missed a couple games because of that. And then the next three after she came back she barely shot a 3. So to still get her 1,000 and to still have that kind of offensive production standing all of 5-foot-1 I think is remarkable.

“Of course her athleticism and her speed make her someone hard to guard. And probably she’s the type of kid that a lot of teams zoned us because her foot speed getting past the defender was so good. But she’s just a such a good, coachable kid. If I’m talking to the locker room or in a huddle, her eyes are locked on me. She’s nodding. She’s just a coach’s dream that never gives you an issue, super competitive. You get a couple Aftin Griffins a lifetime, I feel like. And I was glad to be able to coach her.”

The Kings went 20-4, posted their best regular season in program history and had a last-second loss in double-overtime in their sectional. They were coming off back-to-back sectional titles and a semi-state appearance in 2024.

Griffin will run cross country and track at IU Indianapolis.

Another lightning quick guard for the Lady Kings, the 5-6 senior guard averaged 10.0 ppg, 2.9 apg and 3.9 spg.

Amor said Hedrick was the Lady Kings’ top defender and all-around player overall.

“Anna is our best person to just bug the heck out of the ball,” he said. “She was four steals away from breaking our school record for steals in a season.

“She was incredible. She’s our second all-time in career assists, second all-time in career steals, just a special athlete. And I don’t think Aftin gets her 1,000 points without Anna Hedrick on the floor. She’s been my point guard since her freshman year and I lean pretty heavily on her for floor leadership. It was as she goes, we go kind of thing. She’s just a special kid. She won our MVP this year and deservingly so voted by her teammates. As much as people talk about how great Aftin has been, I think Anna has been just as good for these last four years.”

The 5-7 junior guard averaged 13.0 ppg, 6.9 rpg and 2.9 apg.

“She’s flown under the radar because and rightly so because Kroft got a lot of the headlines and put up the really big numbers,” Stesiak said, “but if you would’ve put Sadie on any other team in the area, I think she would’ve been their leading scorer. She would’ve had a lot of those accolades. And as I told Sadie, she’s right there knocking on the door of everything Kroft has done through their junior years. If you look at a lot of the statistical things, Sadie’s right there on pace and in a few cases a little bit ahead of where Kandace was as a junior. She’s that three-level scorer, she’s got a ton of athleticism and every year she’s kind of developed more to her game.

“Looking to next year, hopefully it’s more just a retool and not a rebuild. It may look different, but there’s some dynamic things that Popejoy can do with the ball in her hands that not a lot of other players can. And she’s not under the radar now. Coaches who’ve coached against her and our coaching staff really appreciate all that she’s done and all that she’s put into it so far. But sort of the keys to the team are hers now.”

The 5-9 senior forward averaged 11.6 ppg and 8.6 rpg.

“Iverson’s just a little bit of an undersized post player, a good athlete, maybe not your traditional post player in having a lot of different post moves, but she has a knack for rebounding and scoring,” Stesiak said. “She just missed the season rebounding record this year. I think she was about 20 away from that. Just her ability to finish on the block and that dynamic of Kroft, Popejoy and Iverson worked really well together because teams had to guard Iverson in the post.

“She could catch a dump down from Kroft or Popejoy, she can get on offensive rebound, she can catch and finish around the basket and then a little bit of that mid-range game. And then she was a great rebounder. So those three really sort of gave us a triple threat that were a big part of our success.”

The 5-7 sophomore averaged 9.3 ppg.

“Grace was another one this year who had a tremendous jump too,” coach Douglass said. “Her scoring average I think will be even higher next year. She led us in made 3-pointers. I think she had 41 this year. She shot the ball better, her percentage was better. At the end of the year we finally got her driving to the basket again and getting to the foul line. That’s gonna be the big key for her next year is to stay aggressive like that. But she’s another girl that was a freshman last year, sophomore this year and she made that jump on the court. Now it’s just getting the consistency from her from game to game. Looking forward to having a tremendous junior year out of her.”

The Berries’ 5-11 senior center averaged 8.1 ppg, 8.3 rpg and 2.9 bpg.

“Goldie continued to progress throughout her senior year,” Logan coach Chris Keisling said. “I’d say she was obviously the kid that we depended upon inside. She consistently was our best scorer. The last game of the season at the sectional kind of epitomized her progress as she ended up getting 20 of our 26 against Western, I think she ended up about six rebounds. And so that’s kind of what she meant to us. We focused on getting the ball inside to her, trying to take some of the pressure off of the guard play out front. And so she just did a nice job improving throughout the year.

“When we look at where she was at her freshman year to her senior year, it just was remarkable. Her footwork got so much better. Her post play as a result of just her improved footwork inside was just much better.”

The Kings’ 5-11 sophomore forward averaged 7.0 ppg and 4.0 rpg

“She was our most improved player,” Amor said. “I could barely get her on the floor at the varsity level her freshman year. And I thought skill-wise, she probably deserved some more time, just physically wasn’t ready and mentally wasn’t ready. And she was our sixth man our first few games this year and then just earned that starting spot. Then it got towards the end of the year where we didn’t want to have her off the floor. She’s a kid that I think our success will lean on her quite a bit these next couple years. But she’s got a supporting cast around her that I think that we we’re not gonna drop off now that these seniors graduated.”

The Panthers’ 5-8 junior guard averaged 6.5 ppg.

“Avery just stepped up and made some big shots for us at times,” McWherter said. “Last year she just did everything for us defensively and didn’t give us a whole lot of offense, but this year she gave us a lot more offense. She made the Logansport Savings Bank team, made the Kitchen Classic team, put up 26 points against Taylor, she had six 3s. She went like 8 for 14 from the field in that game also.

“Her biggest asset for us last year and probably still this year besides shooting the 3s this year was her defensive putting pressure on the ball and playing the top of our zone. We’re really looking forward to having her back for her senior year.”

The 5-9 sophomore averaged 6.4 ppg and 4.8 rpg.

“Allison led us in 3-point percentage and led us in free throw percentage on the year,” coach Douglass said. “Her confidence just grew, especially towards the end of the year. She finally started asserting herself and being aggressive when she had the ball. And watching her grow from last year and being healthy was her biggest thing. This year she had the shoulder surgery over the summer, had a little bit of issues there on and off at the beginning of the year. But once she got confidence back in that shoulder her shooting was tremendous for us. Her ability to go inside for us and score inside and that aggressiveness and strength that she has and just being a sophomore looking at her for next year too and the work that she’ll put in.”

Layer, a 5-3 junior guard, averaged 6.4 ppg and 2.8 spg.

McWherter said he was glad the softball standout came back out for basketball.

“Without her playing basketball this year, we don’t win 10 ballgames, we maybe win six and we don’t get to the sectional championship,” he said. “She just does an outstanding job. She’s kind of our motor. She goes full speed. Another kid that just is very tough, she’s probably got the biggest toughness and the most tenacity for us, and she just completely gives us a ton of effort and her helping with the ball handling responsibilities, taking pressure off of Mia, like I said, if we don’t have her our 10 wins drop down to five or six because she just took so much of the pressure off of Mia.”

The 5-8 sophomore averaged 6.2 ppg and 9.6 rpg.

“Liz is just an athlete,” McWherter said. “She had six double-doubles on the year, had 10 or more rebounds in five of our seven conference games. The big game of the year she had for us was at Oregon-Davis, she had 10 offensive rebounds. She’s just a complete athlete, led us in field goal percentage at 49% on the season, led us in rebounding, just shy of 10 rebounds a game. We’re really look forward to having her for two more years.”

Clem, a 5-8 sophomore, averaged 5.7 ppg, 8.9 rpg and 3 spg.

“Lily’s been a really dependable rebounder for us and defender inside, and she also led us in steals this year,” Keisling said. “She is just very active defensively. We really focused on trying to improve her point production. That’s an area that she hasn’t really been a kid that’s looked to score, but this year we tried to do what we could to improve her scoring output, and she did improve her scoring output and started to look more to score this year which we think will help her as we head into next. But her defense, her rebounding at about 10 a game and her leading us in steals this year, those are the kind of things that she really brings to the table consistently.”

title: McIlroy seeks rare Masters repeat in wide-open Augusta fight
description: Rory McIlroy, whose emotional 2025 Masters victory completed an incredible career grand slam, chases an even rarer feat this year -- repeating as champion at Augusta National.McIlroy joined a career grand slam group that also includes Nicklaus, Woods, Gary Player, Ben Hogan and Gene Sarazen.
fullText:
Rory McIlroy, whose emotional 2025 Masters victory completed an incredible career grand slam, chases an even rarer feat this year -- repeating as champion at Augusta National.

The 36-year-old from Northern Ireland hopes to join Tiger Woods, Jack Nicklaus and Nick Faldo as the only golfers to capture back-to-back green jackets when the 90th Masters tees off Thursday.

"I know defending the Masters tournament is a pretty rare feat, and it's something I'd love to do." McIlroy said.

"I've won it once and I feel like that will make it a bit easier for me to win again."

McIlroy delivered an image for the ages when he fell to his knees last April after ending a 10-year major win drought by defeating Justin Rose in a playoff to win the Masters.

McIlroy joined a career grand slam group that also includes Nicklaus, Woods, Gary Player, Ben Hogan and Gene Sarazen.

Oddsmakers have made top-ranked Scottie Scheffler a slight favorite over world number two McIlroy, even though the defending champion said he has recovered from a back injury that forced him to withdraw last month at Bay Hill.

American Scheffler, a two-time Masters winner, took the American Express title in January and last year's British Open and PGA Championship.

Scheffler, who withdrew from last month's Houston Open as he and his wife are expecting their second child, is working on mental aspects of his game.

"My expectations all are based around what I want mentally," Scheffler said. "So far throughout this season I've been really good in some spots and some other spots I feel like I can improve in terms of my commitment to the shot."

Tiger Woods, a 15-time major winner, will not attend after pleading not guilty to DUI charges stemming from a March 27 car crash.

"I know and understand the seriousness of the situation I find myself in," Woods said. "I am stepping away for a period of time to seek treatment and focus on my health."

Masters chairman Fred Ridley and the PGA Tour supported the 15-time major winner's decision.

Five Americans with multiple major titles seek their first green jacket -- Justin Thomas, Bryson DeChambeau, Brooks Koepka, Xander Schauffele and Collin Morikawa, who withdrew from the PGA's Texas Open with a sore back.

DeChambeau and Jon Rahm from LIV Golf also are fancied by oddsmakers. DeChambeau won titles last month in South Africa and Singapore while Rahm won in Hong Kong.

"I know I have to dial in my wedges. That's going to be a big thing for me," DeChambeau said when asked about the Masters.

"If I'm five more percent consistent, I have a better chance than what I did last year at the Masters."

PGA Valspar Championship winner Matt Fitzpatrick was asked about Masters confidence after his first PGA title in almost three years.

"I'm probably going to win," Fitzpatrick said with a laugh. "No, I mean, I'm obviously very confident in my game right now.

"But what it takes to win a major is very different to what it takes to win on the PGA Tour, particularly the Masters. There's extra pressure on the Masters, no matter who you are. It just has that standing above all the other events, as well as the majors."

England's Fitzpatrick, the 2022 US Open winner, seeks a hot start at Augusta.

"There's stuff I want to improve," he said. "I want to make sure I'm ready for when I get to Augusta and to be ready that Thursday morning."

Players Championship winner Cameron Young said winning at TPC Sawgrass was "the best prep you could ask for."

"My goal is to be prepared to be playing late on Sunday at Augusta... it's to be ready and comfortable when that moment comes."

title: Washington softball falls 8-4 to No. 24 Stanford Cardinal
description: The Washington Huskies dropped consecutive games for just the second time this season in an 8-4 loss to No. 24 Stanford.
fullText:
A trip to Palo Alto, California, was not kind to coach Heather Tarr and the Washington Huskies, who went 1-3 over their four games following an 8-4 loss to the No. 24 Stanford Cardinal on Saturday afternoon.

With ace Morgan Reimer getting a day off after throwing 165 pitches in UW's 3-2 loss to Saint Mary's on Friday, Tarr turned to Arizona transfer Ryan Maddox in the circle, who the Huskies got an early 4-0 lead for. An early run-scoring double from third baseman Giselle Alvarez and an RBI single from freshman Ally Hetzel put the Huskies up 2-0 in the first inning, before center fielder Kaycie Burdick tacked onto the lead with an RBI single of her own in the second inning.

Then, Burdick got caught stealing at second, but allowed right fielder Sophi Mazzola to score from third base on the play to put the Huskies up 4-0 early in the game and give Maddox a nice cushion. However, from that point on, it was all Cardinal.

FINAL | Stanford wins it. pic.twitter.com/laGQslfjOt

— Washington Softball (@UWSoftball) April 4, 2026

Stanford took control of the contest in the bottom of the third inning, putting up five runs in the frame, led off by back-to-back solo home runs from second baseman Addyson Sheppard and left fielder Sydney Boulaphinh before Maddox was lifted for Rylee Rehbein. The Montana transfer was charged with the loss after working 2 1/3 innings and letting up 4 runs (3 earned) on 4 hits and 2 walks with a strikeout.

While the Huskies were able to put together 11 hits in the contest against the Cardinal pitching staff, they couldn't get anything else going in the late innings. Ava Bulanti earned the win for Stanford after she worked 2 2/3 scoreless innings and let up 3 hits with 2 strikeouts, as Washington has now lost three of its last four games and suffered back-to-back losses for just the second time this season.

The loss dropped the Huskies to 31-9 on the year, and now they'll return to Seattle for a three-game series with the Minnesota Golden Gophers, which is set to begin on Friday, April 10, at 7:00 p.m. PT.

This article originally appeared on Huskies Wire: Washington Huskies fall 8-4 to No. 24 Stanford in second straight loss

title: Plate umpire Ron Kulpa leaves Marlins-Yankees game after taking foul ball off mask
description: Plate umpire Ron Kulpa left Saturday’s game between the Miami Marlins and New York Yankees with two outs in the fourth inning after taking a foul ball off his mask.  Kulpa was hit in the mask by a foul ball earlier in the inning and stayed in as Jakob Marsee’s RBI single gave the Marlins a 4-0 lead.
fullText:
NEW YORK (AP) — Plate umpire Ron Kulpa left Saturday’s game between the Miami Marlins and New York Yankees with two outs in the fourth inning after taking a foul ball off his mask.

Kulpa was hit in the mask by a foul ball earlier in the inning and stayed in as Jakob Marsee’s RBI single gave the Marlins a 4-0 lead. After Agustín Ramírez scored on the hit, Kulpa motioned to the Yankees’ dugout and was checked out by two trainers.

After a few minutes, Kulpa exited under his own power for further examination.

The game was delayed about 10 minutes and resumed with Scott Barry moving from first base to the plate.

Kulpa made his umpiring debut in the majors July 23, 1998, and was promoted to a full-time umpire in 1999. He was part of the umpiring crew for the World Series in 2011 and 2021.

AP MLB: https://apnews.com/hub/MLB

title: Dan Hurley NCAA tournament record: How UConn head coach's March dominance compares to coaching legends
description: With his team's latest championship appearance, Hurley's legacy continues to grow in March.
fullText:
Dan Hurley NCAA tournament record: How UConn head coach's March dominance compares to coaching legends originally appeared on The Sporting News. Add The Sporting News as a Preferred Source by clicking here.

Dan Hurley's resume won't stop growing.

At 53 years old and in his eighth season coaching the UConn Huskies, Hurley's unit is once again set to play for a national title. By beating the Illinois Fighting Illini in Saturday's Final Four matchup, the Huskies will soon have a chance to win their third championship in four years.

Hurley isn't just inching toward adding another title to his coaching legacy — the NCAA Tournament success he's attained already has him in conversations with all-time greats.

Here's a look at how Hurley's updated NCAA Tournament record compares to college basketball's best coaches ever.

MARCH MADNESS HQ:Live NCAA bracket | TV schedule | Latest news and more

Saturday's win over UConn was Hurley's 20th NCAA Tournament win in his coaching career, with two of those victories coming at Rhode Island in 2017 and 2018, and the rest as the leader of the Huskies.

Of Hurley's five losses in the tournament, two of them came at Rhode Island. He is in his eighth season at UConn in 2025-26 — and over that stint, the Huskies have made March Madness six times, only being eliminated in three of them as of the 2026 Final Four.

Hurley's record at UConn in the NCAA Tournament is an outstanding 18-3. That includes a record of 11-0 once the Huskies reach the Sweet 16 and 18-1 the last four seasons.

Dan Hurley just keeps winning at UConn. pic.twitter.com/n3lNkugSVE

— The Sporting News (@sportingnews) April 5, 2026

MORE:Full history of UConn's men's basketball championships

Among coaches with at least 20 NCAA Tournament game appearances, Hurley's .800 winning percentage trails only UCLA legend John Wooden, although the other legends on the list have a much longer track record than Hurley's 20 wins.

Still, no matter how you spin the numbers, one thing is clear: Hurley is tracking to be an all-time coaching great in college basketball.

UConn's Dan Hurley is among the best coaches in NCAA Tournament history 😮‍💨 pic.twitter.com/TyoZhJPfy8

— Yahoo Sports (@YahooSports) April 4, 2026

Hurley now has a clean 350 wins in his coaching career entering the 2026 championship game. He's led UConn to 30-plus wins in a season three different times.

Here's a look at Hurley's record by season:

MORE:How UConn leaned on Dan Hurley's positivity to complete comeback vs. Duke

One coach stands alone in all-time NCAA Tournament wins: Duke's Mike Krzyzewski. "Coach K" racked up 101 March Madness over his career, the only men's coach with at least 100.

Plenty of other legends are on the list — longtime UNC and Kansas coach Roy Williams ranks second with 77, while another all-time Tar Heel, Dean Smith, is third with 65.

Hurley, 53, still has a ways to go if he's going to even enter the top-10 for NCAA Tournament wins.

UMass, Memphis, Kentucky, Arkansas

Oral Roberts, Tulsa, Illinois, Kansas

Boston University, Providence, Kentucky, Louisville, Iona, St. John's

MORE: Did Dan Hurley play in the NBA?

Hurley has already brought two national titles to UConn, when the Huskies went back-to-back in 2023 and 2024. The first title may have been more unexpected, but UConn was flat-out dominant in 2023-24, winning a program-record 37 games while cruising to a No. 1 seed and second-straight championship.

Now, Hurley will have a chance to bring his third championship to the Huskies in 2026.

title: UConn reaches 3rd national title game in 4 seasons, beating Illinois 71-62 behind Mullins and Reed
description: Tarris Reed Jr. had 17 points and 11 rebounds, while the fabulous freshman Mullins scored 15 for the Huskies.
fullText:
INDIANAPOLIS — UConn got another critical 3-pointer from Braylon Mullins and coach Dan Hurley’s Huskies are heading back to the national title game, beating Illinois 71-62 in the Final Four on Saturday night as they seek their third championship in four seasons.

Tarris Reed Jr. had 17 points and 11 rebounds, while the fabulous freshman Mullins scored 15 for the Huskies (34-5), who rode strong inside play and tough defense to their 19th straight victory in the Sweet 16 or later rounds of the NCAA Tournament.

UConn will face Arizona or Michigan on Monday night as it seeks its seventh title, all since 1999, and third under Hurley, who would become the only active coach with more than two.

Mullins, whose buzzer-beating 3-pointer sent the Huskies past Duke and into the Final Four, hit a catch-and-shoot 3 with 52 seconds left — his only basket of the second half — to give UConn a 66-59 lead.

Freshman Keaton Wagler had 20 points and eight rebounds to lead the Fighting Illini (28-9), who reached their first Final Four since losing the championship game to North Carolina in 2005.

Wagler and Mullins became the first pair of freshmen to top 15 points in a Final Four game since Michael Jordan and Patrick Ewing in 1982.

UConn’s Alex Karaban had nine points on 1-of-8 shooting while adding four rebounds and four assists. He’s trying to become the first player since John Wooden’s dominant UCLA teams in the 1960s and 1970s to finish his career as a three-time champion. He also tied Hurley’s brother, Bobby, for second on the career list with 18 March Madness victories.

The Huskies haven’t lost a tournament game played past the opening weekend since 2009, when they fell in the national semifinals to Michigan State. With one more victory, they would break a tie with North Carolina and move into third place alone in national titles, trailing only UCLA (11) and Kentucky (eight).

title: Students, fans pack Ann Arbor bars to watch Michigan in Final Four
description: Fans on University of Michigan's campus spent Saturday evening anticipating the Final Four men's basketball game against Arizona.
fullText:
Students and fans dressed in maize and blue gathered at downtown Ann Arbor bars and restaurants ahead of Michigan basketball taking on Arizona in the men’s Final Four game Saturday night.

Lines wrapped around the side of Good Time Charley's and people waited outside Brown Jug and The Garage Bar by 7 p.m.

As University of Michigan men's basketball team prepared to take on the University of Arizona, fans around Ann Arbor and across campus anticipated the game.

Hayden Rometty, a junior at UM, said he was at home in Farmington but came back to campus because he “wouldn’t rather be anywhere else right now.”

He said the other side of his bracket isn’t looking good because he choose Duke, but he’s hopeful his winning choice U-M will pull through with the national championship.

More: Michigan vs Arizona live Final Four updates: Prediction, odds, how to watch

“I think if we play like we did against Tennessee and stay consistent and get on that big run early, if we can get on a big run in the first half, that can make a big difference.”

UM mechanical engineering graduate student Aleksandra Dudek said she had UM winning the men’s bracket and UCLA, her undergraduate Alma matter, winning her women’s bracket. She was excited to watch UM play tonight and hopefully get it closer to two winning brackets.

“It's super fun, and we decided to come out even though it's gonna be hectic,” she said of the atmosphere in Ann Arbor on game day. “We’re still excited to see everyone out here, and hopefully celebrate at the end.”

Paul Scheffler, came to Ann Arbor his wife Jessica Scheffler, 40, to watch the game at a bar after watching the Tiger’s game at Comerica. They drove from Millburg, Michigan, and were excited to be among U-M fans.

“I told her I’ve been coming to Ann Arbor for games to Crisler or the Big House and I’ve never been here to a bar for a big game,” he said.

Freshman Rese Sinawi said U-M is her dream school and she’s hoping the team will win and keep her bracket in good shape.

“It’s very exciting the atmosphere is very high. The streets are crowded. It’s just lot of fun and exciting, Sinawi said.

title: President Donald Trump's executive order sells 1 thing to college sports in NCAA president's eyes
description: NCAA President Charlie Baker spoke before Saturday's Final Four games about the executive order signed by Donald Trump.
fullText:
INDIANAPOLIS — NCAA President Charlie Baker is under no illusions that a recent move from the White House will solve every one of the myriad problems facing his membership.

Baker is hopeful, though, that the executive order signed by President Trump on Friday can act as what it was perhaps intended to be from the beginning: A firm hand in the back of Congress to find a reasonable solution to those problems through legislative action.

If the order does that, it will have managed what nothing else has, in beginning to ease the turmoil plaguing college sports through the last half-decade. Which makes that outcome possible, but difficult to imagine.

“I think part of the message from him,” Baker, referring to President Trump, said, “is, can we find a way to push this a little harder through the legislative process, and get something on the books that represents what most people are looking for at this point? Which is a much simpler eligibility process.”

Eligibility sat at the center of the executive order signed Friday. It sets a range of parameters on that issue — from barring professionals from returning to college, to setting athletes’ eligibility window at five years, to limiting those athletes to one penalty free transfer in their careers.

There are other concerns discussed in a directive many, including those within the executive branch itself, expect to be challenged legally. The order directs the NCAA, per a report from Yahoo! Sports, to “update its rules” accordingly by Aug. 1, which at least allows runway for any potential changes and does not immediately affect college sports’ day-to-day operation.

Baker acknowledged the difficulties associated with some aspects of the order. Not least the directive to bring the association’s rules in line, understandable in theory but difficult in practice when the NCAA is already facing varied challenges to its eligibility rules in the courts.

Still, the NCAA’s fourth-year leader expressed optimism at the potential for President Trump’s order to shake loose enough congressional action to make meaningful progress toward national legislation.

“There are a number of Democrats who said what I would describe as some reasonably positive things about some key elements of it,” Baker said, adding with a smile, “not a lot of people say nice things about much of anything” at the legislative level right now.

What Baker will want it to help specifically is the SCORE Act, legislation introduced in July and still working its way through the House.

Widely supported by NCAA membership, the SCORE Act would carve out a limited antitrust exemption for the association while creating federal standards around issues including name, image and likeness. Both the Big Ten Conference and Indiana University individually endorsed Friday’s executive order as a means of driving the legislative process forward around the SCORE Act specifically.

It has drawn criticism from both sides of the political spectrum, including concerns over everything from athletes’ rights, long-term financial concerns, congressional overreach and more.

Struggling for the required votes in the House, the SCORE Act would almost certainly face an even steeper road in the Senate, where legislators have introduced bipartisan bills on the issue as well.

Whether the President’s executive order can trigger meaningful progress in a process currently struggling for it remains to be seen. Baker’s hopeful comments might not amount to much more than that — hope.

For his membership, it at least represents fresh support for movement in a stalled process, as the NCAA continues to search for better legal and regulatory footing. If it does shake the trees to the point that something finally falls out, it will have accomplished progress nothing and no one else has to this point. Which is another way of saying: Believe it when you see it.

Zach Osterman is IndyStar's IU insider. Listen to Mind Your Banners, our IU Athletics-centric podcast, on Apple Podcasts, Spotify or wherever you get your podcasts. Watch the latest on IndyStar TV: Hoosiers.

This article originally appeared on Indianapolis Star: Trump executive order signals hope for NCAA president Charlie Baker

title: Dan Hurley confused as fans at Final Four start booing him during postgame interview after leading UConn past Illinois
description: Fans at Lucas Oil Stadium in Indianapolis were not excited to hear from Dan Hurley after UConn's win on Saturday night.
fullText:
With a win already secured, Dan Hurley stepped up to talk with Tracy Wolfson on the court at Lucas Oil Stadium on Saturday night.

Quickly, a loud wave of boos filled the stadium in Indianapolis. The UConn head coach looked incredibly confused.

“Are they booing … I don’t know what they’re booing,” Hurley said, breaking from his answer to Wolfson’s question and looking around briefly.

Hurley, somehow, didn’t realize the boos were for him.

"Are they booing? ... I don't know what they're booing." 😂

Dan Hurley speaks with @tracywolfson after the Huskies advance to a third National Championship Game in four seasons pic.twitter.com/9ZhMqjUhKm

— CBS Sports College Basketball 🏀 (@CBSSportsCBB) April 5, 2026

Now, there’s a couple reasons for the harsh reaction.

For starters, the crowd during the first game at the Final Four was overwhelmingly favoring Illinois. Not only is Champaign a roughly two-hour drive away from Indianapolis, but the Illini were playing in their first Final Four since 2005. Illinois fans showed out, and it was very noticeable throughout the broadcast. So with Illinois losing, it makes sense that Illini fans weren’t too happy to have to listen to Hurley give his postgame interview.

But also, Hurley is not the most likable coach in college basketball. He’s an emotional coach who frequently screams at officials or players throughout games, is incredibly animated and at times steps on the court trying to make his point — which is something he did repeatedly during UConn’s 71-62 win over Illinois on Saturday night. Even during their wild comeback win against Duke in the Elite Eight, there was a moment where Hurley actually went head-to-head with referee Roger Ayres and didn’t receive a technical foul.

Dan Hurley went face-to-face with the ref after UConn beat Duke 😭 pic.twitter.com/FrDGBQZI2q

— Fullcourtpass (@Fullcourtpass) March 30, 2026

Hurley is very aware of the backlash he receives for his behavior, too, and he’s not looking for anything.

“I’m not a victim,” Hurley said on Friday before UConn’s Final Four game, via ESPN. “I’ve done everything. I did what I did. We don’t allow victims in our program, and I’m not a 53-year-old man sitting up here like I’m some victim.

“I don't want to waste a lot of time with it because it takes away from the team. But for me, the way I view what we're going into, in the game, when some people, again, view it as a game, just my family, how I was raised in the sport, where I'm from in Jersey, we look at it more like a battle."

The moment came just one day after longtime UConn women’s basketball coach Geno Auriemma was involved in multiple incidents during their loss to South Carolina in the Final Four. Auriemma called out the officiating and Gamecocks head coach Dawn Staley directly in a heated in-game interview on Friday, and then actually had to be pulled back from Staley after a confrontation on the sidelines in the final moment of their game. Auriemma issued an apology on Saturday.

Regardless of how he’s perceived, Hurley has gotten the Huskies back to the national championship game for the third time in the past four seasons. They’ll take on either Michigan or Arizona in the title game on Monday night. If he can pick up one more win, it’ll mark the third championship Hurley has won over that span.

While the boos and harsh fan reactions are likely continue for Hurley, whether that’s on Monday night or beyond, he keeps winning. Clearly, his coaching style is working out just fine.

title: Derek Chisora put on a slugfest for the ages – he’s answered his retirement question
description: Chisora went out on the losing side but delivered a sure-fire fight-of-the-year contender against fellow heavyweight icon Deontay Wilder
fullText:
“I’m sorry, I love you.” Those were the words offered by Shawn Michaels before he Sweet Chin Music’d Ric Flair into what should’ve been a perfect retirement at WrestleMania 24. Deontay Wilder, 18 years on, tried to give Derek Chisora the same poetic sendoff - repeating those exact words - in a slugfest for the ages. Unlike Flair, who at the tender age of 59 backtracked on his retirement and continued to painstakingly wrestle into his 70s, Chisora should accept this 50th fight for what it is: his rightful finale.

It wasn’t the fairytale ending, with Wilder edging out victory by split decision after 12 rounds of spine-tingling, back-and-forth brawling. But Chisora isn’t the fairytale type. He’s a loveable brute with a maniacal smile and, after an up-and-down career, victory on this night didn’t feel imperative for “Delboy” to deliver. Chaos was all his adoring fans asked for, and that’s what they got.

“I’m going to go home with the boss lady and see,” Chisora said, staying a bit coy over whether his 50th fight would be his last before suggesting this has to be his last fight. “I’m going to go home and drop the kids, do the school run.

“Thank you very much, I really appreciate it. Thank you very much, United Kingdom I really appreciate you. I was born in Zimbabwe, I came over when I was 16, I got in trouble and then got addicted to boxing. I didn’t know I was going to get addicted. Thank you very much, I really appreciate it.”

For a man who was allegedly on the precipice of retirement, Chisora felt like he was allowing himself to properly enjoy the last build of his career. He was romanticising the “scumbag” world he was about to step out of, and used his final media duties to tick off something of a Delboy bucket list; whether that was arriving at his press conference on a tank with controversial Reform UK leader Nigel Farage, or calling Piers Morgan a specific profanity to his face on air.

But Chisora also didn’t feel desperate to sell his boxing farewell as legacy-defining. His mantra in fight week was “f*** it, let’s go”; he was done with the talk and was at peace with leaving everything between the ropes. Maybe that’s why London’s O2 Arena wasn’t completely sold out for what proved a fight-of-the-year contender.

Wilder, meanwhile, was hardly a pillar of composure in the days and weeks leading up. His future after fight night was far less clear and, whether it was his blow-up in front of TalkSport host Simon Jordan, 58, over the topic of the Tyson Fury trilogy – one which required security to step in – or his bizarre rant on Morgan’s show where he made allegations about the conception of his child, he didn’t seem like a man with his mind particularly in check. This felt more like a boom-or-bust for Wilder’s career after seeing his force fade in recent years, and his emotions were indicative of that.

Circumstances created a perfect storm for a fight that seemed guaranteed to bring a finish. Wilder, needing a huge performance, had lost four of his last six but of course once boasted the reputation as arguably the hardest-hitting heavyweight to ever live. Chisora promised to empty the tank for better or worse in his last hurrah.

The expectation of a knockout was entrenched from the outset, with Wilder unloading on Chisora before toppling into Delboy on the ropes, refusing to separate. Chisora’s coach darted into the ring to remedy the situation. This was the first round, a sign of the carnage to come.

Chisora knew he had to survive the early attacks, with Wilder’s optimum time to finish expected to come in the first three or four rounds. But Chisora began to turn the tide, starting to pummel Wilder before the end of the fourth. Wilder came back with counter-punches but the Briton was getting his American foe to the corners and unloading serious punishment. The home crowd were vociferous, as “Oh, Derek Chisora” rang from stand to stand.

To Chisora’s dismay, Wilder’s chin stayed strong and soon the 42-year-old began to tire. Then came the round of the year so far; the eighth. Wilder responded to a stiff shot with two clean crosses before punching Chisora through the ropes, scoring the fight’s first knockdown. There was cinema to these three minutes, with Chisora uttering that famous “Heartbreak Kid” line before unleashing what he thought was a legend-killing blow.

Literally and figuratively, Chisora was looking ropey. He beat the count but seemed there for the taking, only for Wilder’s next move – a push – to cost him a point. Chisora was given a crucial few extra seconds to regain his composure and survive the round.

Each man gassing but still throwing bombs, they seemed to trade knockdowns in the 11th, only for both to seemingly be ruled trips after the fact. Their war of attrition, somehow, now looked set to go the distance.

As their battered skulls kept spraying sweat, the final 10 seconds of the final round sent the crowd to a fever pitch one last time. Both searched for a Hail Mary knockout that didn’t come, and the bell sounded on a barnstormer and surely Chisora’s storied career. The result keeps Wilder’s career alive but it didn’t really matter when it came to the hero of the hour. This was the climax Chisora’s journey warranted; an unadulterated firefight.

Doubt will still hover over the definitiveness of his boxing departure, because of Chisora’s unpredictable and sometimes irrational character. But this should answer his retirement question once and for all. He’s made his money and “Delboy”, despite his true love for the game still burning, just couldn’t deny that this is how his time in the ring was meant to end. "You know when it's time, it's time.”

title: Brazil U17s thrash Bolivia in South American opener
description: Kauê Furquim celebrates his second goal of the matchCredits: Nelson Terme / CBFBrazil's Under-17 National Team got off to the best possible start in the South American Championship for the category. ...
fullText:
Kauê Furquim celebrates his second goal of the matchCredits: Nelson Terme / CBF

Brazil's Under-17 National Team got off to the best possible start in the South American Championship for the category. In a match played this Saturday (4) at Estádio Ameliano Villeta, in Villeta, Paraguay, the team coached by Carlos Eduardo Patetuci defeated Bolivia 5-0. Eduardo Conceição, Kauê Furquim (3) and Eduardo Pape scored the goals.

With the result, Brazil's Under-17 National Team reached three points and sits at the top of Group B. Their next match is against Peru on Monday (6) at 8 p.m., once again at Ameliano Villeta.

Eduardo Conceição and Kauê Furquim celebrate Brazil's first goal of the matchCredits: Nelson Terme / CBF

Just four minutes in, Brazil opened the scoring. After a good attacking move, Eduardo Conceição found the back of the net.

The goal put Bolivia on alert, and they tried a few attacks, but ran into goalkeeper Vitor, who was always attentive.

Calmly, Brazil imposed its rhythm and managed to extend the lead in the 27th minute through Kauê Furquim, who combined well with his teammates before celebrating his goal.

And it was all Brazil in the match. In the 31st minute, Eduardo Pape met a good cross from the left and headed it firmly into the back of the net.

There was still time for another goal in the first half. Kauê Furquim struck powerfully to score his second of the match. Brazil went into halftime with a 4-0 lead.

Brazil did not take its foot off the gas. Five minutes into the second half, the team coached by Carlos Eduardo Patetuci put together another fine move down the right, and who was there to find the net? Him again: Kauê Furquim, who scored his third goal of the match.

Even with a comfortable lead, Brazil did not stop attacking. The team created good plays down the flanks, both through Samuel and Arthur Monteiro, but goalkeeper Ronald Roel made good saves and prevented Brazil from adding to the score. And the match ended with a 5-0 win for the Seleção.

Brazil made an emphatic start in the South American U-17 ChampionshipCredits: Nelson Terme /CBF

title: Lady Vols defeat South Carolina to win SEC softball series
description: Lady Vols win series finale versus South Carolina.
fullText:
No. 7 Tennessee (32-6, 9-6 SEC) defeated South Carolina (23-17, 3-9 SEC), 4-2, Saturday at Sherri Parker Lee Stadium.

The Lady Vols won the three-game SEC series against the Gamecocks.

Tennessee led, 3-0, after scoring three runs in the second inning. Tennessee scored its first run of the contest after Maddi Rutan recorded a RBI when she reached on a fielder's choice that scored Meredith Barnhart.

The Lady Vols extended their advantage to, 2-0, after Gabby Leach reached on a fielder's choice and Saviya Morgan scored on the play.

Tennessee added one run in the third inning after Alannah Leach hit a solo home run, making the score, 4-0.

South Carolina scored both of its runs in the fourth inning.

Alannah Leach went 2-for-3. She hit one home run, recording one RBI and one run, while Dodge went 1-for-3. She hit one triple.

Karlyn Pickens (10-4) earned a win in relief. She pitched 3.2 innings and allowed two hits. Pickens recorded five strikeouts, while totaling 51 pitches, including 36 strikes.

Sage Mardjetko started for the Lady Vols and pitched 3.1 innings, allowing two runs (one earned) and two hits. She recorded three strikeouts, while totaling 49 pitches, including 32 strikes.

title: Mariners Game #9 Preview and Discussion: SEA at LAA, 4/4
description: Hancock on the hill in Anaheim
fullText:
Here’s something I never thought I’d say: I am excited to watch Emerson Hancock pitch tonight.

The Mariners look to win their first series of the season Saturday against the Angels. They won Friday’s opener 4-0 in a long, frustrating, 10-inning game that didn’t really get going until the end. The bats did eventually come alive, just in time to ruin the Angels’ home opener.

Hancock will take the mound looking to make it two in a row for the Mariners and for himself. Hancock threw six no-hit innings last week against the Guardians, striking out nine in the process. He seemed to pick right up where he left off after switching to the bullpen at the end of last year, with improved velocity capable of generating a competitive number of whiffs.

One change he made in that first start was lowering his arm slot even further, continuing his trend from his brief days as a reliever. The improved Stuff+ numbers held in his first start of 2026. With three kinds of fastballs, a changeup, and a sweeper — all thrown from a low arm slot — Hancock now has all the makingss of a contemporary starter, even if “just OK” velocity limits him to a mid-rotation arm. There’s still a lot more to prove, of course, but the lack of depth in the Mariners’ rotation last year is what kept them from a more impressive win total. Hancock pitching anywhere above replacement level would be a crucial development early in 2026.

The Mariners will face Angels’ righty Jack Kochanowicz, who Jake Mailhot covered in our series preview. Brendan Donovan is out of the lineup after exiting Friday’s game with a leg injury after stepping awkwardly on first base while trying to leg out a grounder. Leo Rivas takes his place at third, and Luke Raley moves up to the leadoff spot.

Brendan Donovan has been announced as day-to-day with a groin injury. He got imaging yesterday and the team will review before offering an update. He is not in tonight’s lineup.

Brennen Davis (hamstring) and Victor Labrada (oblique) have been activated in Tacoma. Both are expected to play tonight.

title: Riley Reese’s overtime winner seals No. 12 Maryland men’s lacrosse’s thrilling 8-7 win over No. 9 Ohio State
description: Reese’s first-career goal sealed a stunning Senior Day victory
fullText:
It was only fitting that No. 12 Maryland men’s lacrosse’s game-winning goal against No. 9 Ohio State came from defender Riley Reese on Senior Day.

After three seasons on the bench — appearing in 10 games during that stretch — Reese was cast into the starting role following a gruesome injury to Will Schaller. All hope seemed lost for the Terps with their top defender likely out for the rest of the season.

But the senior has quickly settled into Maryland’s defensive trio, finding an instant connection with Eric Spanos. Reese’s first career point came during the Terps’ rout of Michigan, setting up Spanos for one of his career-high eight goals.

That duo linked up once 

Reese’s first career goal — and shot on goal — propelled Maryland to an 8-7 victory over Ohio State for its first ranked win of the season.

Against the nation’s top defense, goals were always going to be hard to come by for the Terps’ attack. Maryland has scored single-digit goals three times this season and shown vulnerability to poor offensive showings.

However, the Terps could not have imagined a better start to Saturday’s game. After a 14-goal showing last week, Maryland strung together a strong opening six minutes. It was the best the offense has looked all season.

Quick ball movement allowed the Terps to find open attackers around the crease. They capitalized. Maryland scored on four of its first five shots on cage, each from a different goalscorer.

That balanced offense was fueled by Leo Johnson. The senior attacker has been at his best this season creating the Terps’ offense. His incredible vision and passing have opened up quality looks for teammates, making opposing defenses look silly at times.

In the first quarter, Johnson wreaked havoc. He contributed to three of Maryland’s first four goals, including a nifty one-on-one move on his defender.

After receiving a feed from Spanos at the top of the attacking zone, Johnson cut inside before spinning back to his strong hand. That space was all he needed. The Yale transfer roofed it into the top corner past Ohio State goalie Caleb Fyock.

With the Terps’ offense humming early on, last season’s All-American first-teamer could do nothing about it. Fyock managed just one stop early on, fueling Maryland’s second five-goal first quarter of the season.

But Maryland scored just two goals in the final 45 minutes of regulation — one of which came on a ridiculous pass from Johnson to Aiden Aitken for his career-high second goal of the game.

Fyock quickly settled in after his slow start, stopping 13 of the Terps’ final 17 shots on goal. He finished with an impressive 14 saves and 63.6% save percentage to fuel Ohio State’s comeback.

After trailing 4-0 six minutes into the game, the Buckeyes slowly chipped away at Maryland’s strong lead. A 14-minute scoring drought from the Terps late in the first and into the second quarter didn’t help either.

Ohio State scored twice in the final two minutes before halftime, adding to Brian Ruppel’s shaky outing. The senior netminder managed just four first-half stops and conceded a goal he’d like to have back, allowing a harmless shot to roll into the far post.

But with Maryland’s offense continuously stalling out — it had four turnovers in the third quarter — Ruppel made some critical stops in the second half. He ended with 10 saves for his seventh double-digit performance.

None were bigger than his last save of the night. With the game tied at seven and two seconds remaining in the fourth quarter, Ruppel denied Garrett Haas at the near post on his close-range effort.

That stop pushed the game into overtime, setting up Reese’s heroics.

1. Maryland controls the X. In a matchup that featured two of the nation’s top-10 faceoff units, extra possessions were going to be pivotal in Saturday’s game. That rang true. Maryland corralled eight of the first 10 faceoffs to fuel its early start. But Ohio State’s rejuvenation at the X allowed it to climb back. Still, the Terps won 12 of the 18 faceoffs.

2. Eric Spanos held silent. After a career day in Maryland’s rout of Michigan, Spanos was a nonfactor in Saturday’s game. The senior attacker was held off the scoresheet in the first half and took a backseat approach as Johnson created the Terps’ offense. But his lone contribution came in the biggest moment of the game.

3. Offense sputters late. With its top scorers held in check during the second half, Maryland’s attack failed to generate many quality looks. The Terps often waited until late in the shot clock to get downhill, forcing them into some errant long-range efforts.

title: Houston Rockets jersey history No. 10 - Eric Gordon (2019-23)
description: During his time suiting up for the Rockets, Gordon wore only jersey No. 10 and put up 15.7 points, 2.3 rebounds, and as many assists per game.
fullText:
The Houston Rockets have had players donning a total of 52 different jersey numbers (and have one not part of any numerical series for Houston assistant coach and general manager Carroll Dawson) since their founding at the start of the 1967-68 season, worn by just under 500 players in the course of Rockets history.

To honor all of the players who wore those numbers over the decades, Rockets Wire is covering the entire history of jersey numbers and the players who wore them since the founding of the team all those years ago right up to the present day.

With seven of those jerseys now retired to honor some of the greatest Rockets of all time to wear those jerseys, there is a lot of history to cover.

And for today's article, we will continue with the 20th of 21 who wore the No. 10, guard alum Eric Gordon. After ending his college career at Indiana, Gordon was picked up with the seventh overall selection of the 2008 NBA Draft by the Los Angeles Clippers.

The Indianapolis, Indiana native played the first three seasons of his pro career with the Clippers. He also played for the (then) New Orleans Hornets (now, Pelicans) before he signed with the Houston Rockets in 2019. His stay with the team lasted until he was dealt back to the Clips in 2023.

During his time suiting up for the Rockets, Gordon wore only jersey No. 10 and put up 15.7 points, 2.3 rebounds, and as many assists per game.

All stats and data courtesy of Basketball Reference.
